Spruce Root

Bioregional Observatory/4 Bioregional Organizations/Spruce Root.md
Activities
33314415-10e1-8102-9cd3-f85663979688, 33314415-10e1-8157-90d0-e49fb412f4b1, 33314415-10e1-815e-bc4a-c4d52f921ebb, 33314415-10e1-814f-830d-cd24c8cadad4, 33314415-10e1-8140-b65a-d3c731b5498c, 33314415-10e1-81f4-b865-d0e9ae788d92, 33314415-10e1-815b-8147-d64eabbe769c
type
organization
location_lat
58.3
background_section
## Background Southeast Alaska is a coastal and island-rich region spanning over [500 miles from Yakutat in the north to Hydaburg in the south](https://www.biofi.earth/biofi-book). This bioculturally significant area is the ancestral and present-day homeland of the Tlingit, Haida, and Tsimshian peoples, whose governance, economies, and lifeways are deeply tied to the region's forests, salmon streams, and marine ecosystems—relationships forged over thousands of years. The region includes part of the Tongass National Forest, the largest temperate rainforest in North America, and faces a complex legacy of colonial land policies, resource extraction, and economic displacement. ### Origins and Network In 2012, Spruce Root emerged in Juneau, Alaska, as an Indigenous-led Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) with a mission that went far beyond traditional lending. Founded with [$500,000 in seed funding](https://www.biofi.earth/biofi-book) from Sealaska Corporation, one of the regional Alaska Native corporations created by the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, Spruce Root was born from a vision of economic empowerment that honored the deep relationships between people, place, and prosperity. That initial funding came from an unusual source: carbon credits from [176,000 acres of Tongass rainforest](https://www.biofi.earth/biofi-book) that Sealaska had set aside for carbon sequestration in partnership with The Nature Conservancy. After dominating the timber industry in the region until 2021, when Sealaska renounced commercial logging, this marked a profound shift—from extraction to regeneration, from short-term profit to long-term stewardship. Today, Spruce Root serves [23 communities](https://www.biofi.earth/biofi-book) across Southeast Alaska, from Yakutat in the north to Hydaburg in the south.
Sources
33314415-10e1-81a6-9336-c5622668d02e, 33314415-10e1-81e3-8688-e3c9d34559c9, 33314415-10e1-8158-826d-fa47f9253138

Juneau, AK, United States
place
Juneau, Alaska, USA
activities
Small Business Lending|Business Coaching & Programs|Path to Prosperity|Resilience Circles|Sustainable Southeast Partnership|Strategic Planning|Seacoast Trust
description
Spruce Root is an Indigenous-led Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI) serving 23 communities, building a regenerative regional economy by supporting Alaska Native and rural entrepreneurs.
id
spruce-root
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy What Spruce Root has built over more than a decade is something rare: a financial institution that sees its role not just as providing capital, but as cultivating economic sovereignty rooted in place and culture. They've created pathways for Indigenous entrepreneurs to build businesses that honor their values, strengthen their communities, and ensure long-term resilience. Their work recognizes that in Southeast Alaska—a region with a complex legacy of colonial land policies, resource extraction, and economic displacement—economic development must be regenerative, not extractive. It must honor the 10,000-plus years of Indigenous history, traditions, and stewardship. It must build businesses that strengthen communities, not businesses that extract wealth and leave. From the $900,000 in loans deployed in 2023 to the 23 communities served across 500 miles, from the Resilience Circles supporting entrepreneurs' well-being to the Seacoast Trust's vision of $100 million in permanent funding—Spruce Root is demonstrating what bioregional finance can look like when it's Indigenous-led, place-rooted, and values-aligned. They're showing that economic development doesn't have to mean choosing between prosperity and place, between profit and principles. In Southeast Alaska, they're building an economy where businesses succeed by strengthening communities, where financial success is measured by the health of the land and the well-being of the people who call it home.
website
https://www.spruceroot.org/
bioregional_context
Southeast Alaska is a coastal and island-rich region spanning over 500 miles from Yakutat in the north to Hydaburg in the south. This bioculturally significant area is the ancestral and present-day homeland of the Tlingit, Haida, and Tsimshian peoples, whose governance, economies, and lifeways are deeply tied to the region's forests, salmon streams, and marine ecosystems.
